COMPLIANCE DOCUMENT FOR COPYWRITERS & DESIGNERS IN THE FAT LOSS / HEALTH NICHE



Abstract: This standard operating procedure reviews the two elements of effective ad creation: 



a) The creation of compliant “ad copy”; and 

b) The creation of “complaint images”



 “Ad Copy” Creation Guidelines  



FACEBOOK’S POLICIES



Link to FB Ad policy

https://www.facebook.com/policies/ads/#restricted_content





You Cannot Use ‘Negative Personal Attributes’ or ‘Personal Attributes’ in The Copy



FB Policy Extract

Restricted content: Content that asserts or implies personal attributes or negative personal attributes. This includes direct or indirect assertions or implications about a person’s race, ethnic origin, religion, beliefs, age, sexual orientation or practices, gender identity, disability, medical condition (including physical or mental health), financial status, membership in a trade union, criminal record, or name.



Examples of Negative Personal Attributes



You cannot say  

“If you’re serious about getting into shape and losing your extra fat”



You can say 

“If you’re serious about getting into shape and losing extra fat”



Examples of Personal Attributes



You cannot say  

“Other women like you have success using this program”. 



You can say

“Women have success following this program”



Examples Personal Attributes (non-weight loss related)



note: Users in the customized metabolic funnel have offered the information on their personal attributes and I am waiting for confirmation when answers are voluntarily given and are then used to customize questions. 



You Cannot Use Negative Self Imagery In the Copy



FB Policy Extract

Restricted Content: Shocking, sensational, disrespectful, or excessively violent content. Ads that negatively affect our relationship with our users or that promote content, services, or activities, contrary to our competitive position, interests, or advertising philosophy



Examples of Negative Self Imagery 



You cannot say  

“that is why your body is holding on to all that extra flab or worse, actually causing you to get fatter and aging you faster”



You can say 

This program can help your body become fitter, healthier and more vibrant”  



Note: we want to avoid eliciting a user’s negative emotions and instead try to elicit positive emotions from using the product. 





You Cannot Use False Promises In The Copy



FB Policy Extract

Deceptive, false, or misleading content, including deceptive claims, offers, or business practices. / ads that negatively affect our relationship with our users or that promote content, services, or activities, contrary to our competitive position.







Examples of False Promises



You cannot say

“you will double your fat loss … and keep it off forever” 



You can say 

This program can help support you in accelerating the rate at which you burn fat





Testimonials – While Facebook doesn’t specifically mention testimonials in their policy, FB accounts have been flagged for not adhering to the FTC stance on testimonials (described below)



Endorsements must reflect the typical experience of consumers who use the product – not the experience of just a few satisfied customers. If an endorsement doesn’t meet this requirement, the ad must clearly disclose either what consumers can expect their results to be or the limited applicability of the endorser’s experience. It’s not enough to simply add a disclaimer like “Not all consumers will get these results” or “Your results may vary.”





You cannot say

 “Jane lost 30 pounds in 30 days using this program”



You can say 

 “Jane lost 30 pounds over 12 weeks by following this exercise and diet program.





FTC NOTES ON WEIGHT LOSS CLAIMS 

Substantiation:

FTC encourages uses a minimum 12 week double blind placebo controlled study to substantiate a weight loss claim. The placebo effect is said to wear off by 8 weeks, so we should never use a study that is 8 weeks or less for weight loss claims. 

To make a number of pounds lost claim in a headline, we would want 2 good studies showing similar number of pounds lost. This would help prove that the results can be replicated and are likely to happen to the consumers of the product. 

If the participants of the study had any sort of dietary restrictions and/or exercise requirements to do throughout the study, that needs to be disclosed in the copy. 

Potential Claims: 

[Use “may” when the research is tentative, in animals, in vitro, or we don’t have the researched dose]

Helps support your weight loss program/efforts (needs human study)

Promotes weight loss when combined with diet and exercise (needs human study)

Supports healthy weight management

Helps boost/support your metabolism

What does FTC consider obvious weight loss "red flags"?

Cause weight loss of two pounds or more a week for a month or more without dieting or exercise

Cause substantial weight loss no matter what or how much the consumer eats

Cause permanent weight loss (even when the consumer stops using product)

Block the absorption of fat or calories to enable consumers to lose substantial weight

Safely enable consumers to lose more than three pounds per week for more than four weeks

Cause substantial weight loss for all users

Cause substantial weight loss by wearing it on the body or rubbing it into the skin
